network.overall_strategy: This network constructs valine (2-amino-3-methylbutanoic acid, C5H11NO2) from simple C1 feedstocks (CO2, H2, NH3, H2S) through three terminal routes: (1) Strecker synthesis via isobutyraldehyde, (2) reductive amination of alpha-ketoisovalerate, and (3) transamination of alpha-ketoisovalerate with alanine. Hub molecules CO, acetaldehyde, pyruvate, HCN, and alpha-ketoisovalerate create a densely interconnected network. Hydrothermal reactions produce C1-C3 building blocks via greigite/magnetite-catalyzed CO2 reduction and Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis. Surface reactions drive Strecker aminonitrile formation and acetolactate condensation on montmorillonite clay. Biochemical-stage reactions accomplish reductive amination and transamination. Two independent CO sources (direct reverse water-gas shift and formate dehydration) and two independent acetaldehyde sources (FTT synthesis and pyruvate decarboxylation) ensure convergent redundancy at every hub.

reactions.name: Greigite-catalyzed CO2 hydrogenation to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, pH 9-11, H2-rich serpentinization fluid

reactions.mechanism: CO2 adsorbs onto the Fe3S4 surface (structural analog of ferredoxin [Fe4S4] clusters), where surface-bound hydride from dissociative H2 adsorption transfers to CO2 carbon, reducing it to formate (HCOO-). The reaction is thermodynamically favorable under high H2 fugacity conditions at alkaline vents.
reactions.reasoning: Greigite-catalyzed CO2 reduction to formate is well-established as a core reaction of the alkaline hydrothermal vent hypothesis. Roldan et al. (2015) demonstrated CO2 reduction on greigite surfaces, and Preiner et al. (2020) showed H2-dependent CO2 fixation on FeNiS minerals. This provides the first C1 building block for all downstream organic synthesis.

reactions.name: Formate thermal dehydration to CO on mackinawite

conditions.other: subseafloor hydrothermal, analogous to CODH/ACS pathway

reactions.mechanism: Formate adsorbed on mackinawite surface undergoes thermal dehydration at elevated temperature, losing water to produce CO. This mimics the formate-to-CO step in the biological Wood-Ljungdahl (acetyl-CoA) pathway where CODH converts formate to CO on an FeNiS active site.
reactions.reasoning: The formate-to-CO conversion is an essential step in the acetyl-CoA pathway analog proposed by Martin and Russell (2007). Mackinawite (FeS) and its Ni-doped variants have been shown to facilitate carbonyl chemistry. Huber and Wächtershäuser (1997) demonstrated CO-based carbon fixation on FeNiS surfaces. This reaction ensures formate is not a dead-end product and provides CO for downstream FTT and pyruvate synthesis.

reactions.name: Reverse water-gas shift on magnetite

conditions.other: black smoker proximal zone, higher temperature regime

reactions.mechanism: CO2 + H2 → CO + H2O via heterogeneous catalysis on magnetite (Fe3O4) surfaces. Magnetite provides surface oxygen vacancies that activate CO2, while H2 dissociatively adsorbs to provide reducing equivalents. This is the classic reverse water-gas shift (RWGS) reaction.
reactions.reasoning: The reverse water-gas shift on iron oxide surfaces is well-documented in Fischer-Tropsch chemistry literature. McCollom and Seewald (2006) discussed CO generation from CO2 in hydrothermal systems. This reaction provides an alternative, parallel route to CO independent of the formate pathway (rxn_001 + rxn_002), creating convergent redundancy at the CO hub.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of C2-C4 aldehydes

conditions.other: serpentinization zone with native iron, Anderson-Schulz-Flory distribution produces C1-C4+ products

reactions.mechanism: CO and H2 undergo stepwise carbon chain growth on magnetite/Fe0 surfaces via CO insertion and hydrogenation. The Anderson-Schulz-Flory distribution yields a mixture of alcohols, aldehydes, and hydrocarbons. Acetaldehyde (C2) is a major product and isobutyraldehyde (C4, branched) is a minor but significant product, as branching occurs via secondary chain growth mechanisms.
reactions.reasoning: Fischer-Tropsch-type (FTT) synthesis under hydrothermal conditions has been demonstrated by McCollom et al. (1999) and Rushdi and Simoneit (2001), who showed that CO + H2 on magnetite and native iron surfaces produce linear and branched aldehydes, alcohols, and hydrocarbons. Friedmann and Miller (1969) identified isobutyraldehyde as a key precursor for valine via the Strecker reaction in their classic prebiotic synthesis experiments. The co-production of acetaldehyde and isobutyraldehyde in a single FTT reaction is consistent with the Anderson-Schulz-Flory product distribution.

reactions.name: Carbonyl-sulfide-driven pyruvate synthesis

conditions.other: FeS + H2S system, analogous to Wächtershäuser iron-sulfur world; multiple CO insertion steps on mineral surface forming C3 keto acid

reactions.mechanism: CO binds to FeS/NiS surfaces where sequential carbonyl insertion reactions build the C3 backbone. The first CO insertion forms a surface-bound C1 thioester (analogous to acetyl-CoA pathway), a second CO insertion and hydrogenation yields a C2 species (acetyl thioester), and a third carbonyl insertion followed by hydrolysis yields pyruvate (C3 alpha-keto acid). H2S acts as both sulfur donor for surface regeneration and electron shuttle.
reactions.reasoning: Cody et al. (2000) demonstrated the synthesis of pyruvate and other alpha-keto acids from CO under hydrothermal conditions (250°C, 200 MPa) in the presence of FeS. Keefe et al. (1995) showed amino acid synthesis from CO2 with FeS/H2S as reducing agent. This multi-step surface-catalyzed process avoids the problematic single-step CO2-to-pyruvate oversimplification. The iron-sulfur world hypothesis of Wächtershäuser (1988, 1990) provides the theoretical framework for surface-mediated carbon fixation through sequential CO insertions.

reactions.name: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde

conditions.other: non-oxidative decarboxylation, produces acetaldehyde and CO2

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes thermal decarboxylation on the pyrite surface. The alpha-keto acid loses CO2 from the carboxyl group adjacent to the carbonyl, yielding acetaldehyde (CH3CHO). Pyrite surface Lewis acid sites (Fe2+) coordinate the carboxylate and carbonyl oxygens, lowering the activation energy for decarboxylation.
reactions.reasoning: Thermal decarboxylation of alpha-keto acids is a well-known reaction that occurs readily at hydrothermal temperatures. This reaction is significant because it provides a second, independent source of acetaldehyde (complementing FTT synthesis in rxn_004), creating convergent redundancy at the acetaldehyde hub. Pyruvate decarboxylation has been studied in hydrothermal systems by Cody et al. (2000) and is considered prebiotically facile.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal HCN formation from NH3 and CO

conditions.other: black smoker high-temperature zone; NH3 + CO → HCN + H2O is thermodynamically favorable above 500K

reactions.mechanism: At elevated temperatures near black smoker vents, ammonia reacts with carbon monoxide on pyrite surfaces. NH3 is activated by surface coordination, and the N-H bonds are progressively cleaved as the nitrogen forms a triple bond with carbon. The net reaction NH3 + CO → HCN + H2O is driven by the high temperature and the thermodynamic stability of the C≡N triple bond.
reactions.reasoning: HCN formation from simple molecules under hydrothermal conditions has been discussed by Tian et al. (2011) and Rimmer and Rugheimer (2019). While HCN is often assumed to be delivered exogenously (e.g., atmospheric photochemistry), hydrothermal generation provides an endogenous source. This reaction makes HCN a true hub intermediate (produced in-network) rather than an assumed exogenous input, and ensures HCN connects back to CO, completing the network topology. Pyrite catalysis of N-containing species has been discussed in the context of the iron-sulfur world (Wächtershäuser, 1988).

reactions.name: Acetolactate formation via pyruvate-acetaldehyde condensation

conditions.other: evaporitic pool, mildly alkaline pH 7-9, concentrated solutions from partial evaporation; analogous to biological acetolactate synthase reaction

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes nucleophilic addition to acetaldehyde in a mixed aldol-type condensation. The enolate of pyruvate (or its equivalent tautomer) attacks the carbonyl carbon of acetaldehyde, forming the C-C bond of acetolactate (2-acetolactate, 2-hydroxy-2-methyl-3-oxobutanoic acid). Montmorillonite clay provides surface Lewis acid sites that activate the carbonyl groups and concentrate reactants via adsorption.
reactions.reasoning: The condensation of pyruvate and acetaldehyde to form acetolactate is the prebiotic analog of the first step in the biosynthetic valine pathway catalyzed by acetolactate synthase. This reaction connects hydrothermal products (pyruvate from rxn_005, acetaldehyde from rxn_004 or rxn_006) to the surface environment. Clay mineral catalysis of aldol-type condensations has been demonstrated by Gururani et al. and others. The reaction is thermodynamically feasible at surface temperatures in concentrated evaporitic pools.

reactions.name: UV-assisted decarboxylative rearrangement of acetolactate to alpha-ketoisovalerate

conditions.other: UV irradiation (lambda < 300 nm, no ozone layer), surface pool; oxidative decarboxylation and methyl migration

reactions.mechanism: Acetolactate undergoes decarboxylation with concomitant 1,2-methyl migration to yield alpha-ketoisovalerate (3-methyl-2-oxobutanoic acid) plus CO2. In the biosynthetic pathway, this is catalyzed by ketol-acid reductoisomerase. Prebiotically, TiO2 photocatalysis can facilitate the oxidative decarboxylation, and the rearrangement is thermodynamically downhill. UV irradiation provides the activation energy for the concerted decarboxylation-rearrangement.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction is the prebiotic analog of the acetolactate-to-ketoisovalerate conversion in the valine biosynthetic pathway. While enzymatic catalysis achieves this readily, the thermal/photochemical version is plausible given the strong UV flux on early Earth (no ozone layer) and TiO2 photocatalysis. TiO2 photocatalysis of organic transformations has been demonstrated in prebiotic contexts (Ritson and Sutherland, 2012). The decarboxylative rearrangement creates the branched C5 skeleton of alpha-ketoisovalerate, the immediate keto acid precursor to valine.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis step 1: valine aminonitrile formation

conditions.other: evaporitic pool with concentrated HCN and NH3, mildly acidic to neutral pH 5-7

reactions.mechanism: Isobutyraldehyde reacts with ammonia to form the corresponding imine (Schiff base), (CH3)2CH-CH=NH. The imine then undergoes nucleophilic addition by cyanide (CN-) to yield the alpha-aminonitrile, 2-amino-3-methylbutanenitrile. Montmorillonite clay concentrates reactants and provides an acidic surface that promotes imine formation.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker synthesis is the most well-established prebiotic route to alpha-amino acids, including valine. Friedmann and Miller (1969) demonstrated valine synthesis via the Strecker reaction using isobutyraldehyde, HCN, and NH3 under simulated primitive Earth conditions. Miller's spark discharge experiments (1953, 1957) also produced valine among other amino acids via Strecker intermediates. The aminonitrile intermediate is well-characterized in prebiotic chemistry (Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis step 2: aminonitrile hydrolysis to valine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling in evaporitic pool; hydrolysis of nitrile to amide then to carboxylic acid; mildly acidic conditions pH 4-6 during wet phase

reactions.mechanism: 2-amino-3-methylbutanenitrile undergoes stepwise hydrolysis: the nitrile group (-CN) is first hydrated to an amide (-CONH2, giving valine amide), then further hydrolyzed to the carboxylic acid (-COOH), yielding valine. Wet-dry cycling provides alternating conditions: the wet phase allows hydrolysis, while drying concentrates products and drives equilibrium toward hydrolysis completion.
reactions.reasoning: Aminonitrile hydrolysis to amino acids is the second step of the classical Strecker synthesis and is well-established in prebiotic chemistry. Friedmann and Miller (1969) demonstrated the complete Strecker route to valine. The hydrolysis is facilitated by acidic conditions and elevated temperatures during dry phases. Montmorillonite clay can catalyze nitrile hydrolysis through surface acid sites (Ferris et al., 1996). Wet-dry cycling as a driver for prebiotic reactions has been extensively discussed by Damer and Deamer (2020).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of alanine aminonitrile from acetaldehyde

conditions.other: evaporitic pool, mildly acidic to neutral pH 5-7, co-occurs with valine aminonitrile formation in rxn_010

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde reacts with ammonia to form ethylideneamine (CH3CH=NH), which undergoes nucleophilic addition by cyanide to yield 2-aminopropionitrile (alanine aminonitrile). Mechanism is identical to rxn_010 but with the simpler C2 aldehyde substrate.
reactions.reasoning: Alanine is one of the most abundant amino acids produced in Miller-type experiments and Strecker syntheses (Miller, 1953; Parker et al., 2011). Producing alanine in the network serves two purposes: (1) it provides the amino group donor for the transamination route to valine (rxn_015), and (2) it demonstrates the generality of the Strecker pathway across different aldehyde substrates. This reaction consumes HCN, contributing to its hub connectivity.

reactions.name: Alanine aminonitrile hydrolysis to alanine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling, mildly acidic pH 4-6, same pool conditions as rxn_011

reactions.mechanism: 2-aminopropionitrile undergoes stepwise hydrolysis of the nitrile group through the amide intermediate to yield alanine (2-aminopropanoic acid). Mechanism is identical to rxn_011 but with the C3 aminonitrile substrate.
reactions.reasoning: Alanine formation via Strecker hydrolysis is among the best-characterized prebiotic amino acid syntheses (Miller, 1953; Friedmann and Miller, 1969). Alanine produced here serves as the amino group donor for transamination in rxn_015. This reaction ensures the alanine aminonitrile is not a dead-end intermediate.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of alpha-ketoisovalerate to valine

conditions.other: prebiotic pool with available reductant (e.g., Fe2+, NADH analog); pH 6-8; analogous to modern glutamate dehydrogenase reaction

reactions.mechanism: Alpha-ketoisovalerate reacts with ammonia to form the corresponding imine (Schiff base), 3-methyl-2-iminobutanoic acid. The imine is then reduced to valine by available reductants such as Fe2+ or organic hydride donors. This is the reverse of oxidative deamination and is thermodynamically favorable under reducing, NH3-rich conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of alpha-keto acids is the primary route to amino acids in modern biochemistry (via transaminases and glutamate dehydrogenase). Prebiotically, this reaction has been discussed by Bada and Lazcano (2003) and is supported by the observation that alpha-keto acids can be abiotically converted to amino acids using cyanide and ammonia sources (as reviewed in the prebiotic chemistry literature). Keefe et al. (1995) explored FeS/H2S-mediated reductive amination. This provides the second terminal route to valine.

reactions.name: Transamination of alpha-ketoisovalerate with alanine to yield valine

conditions.catalyst: metal ions (Fe2+, Cu2+) as Lewis acid catalysts
conditions.other: prebiotic pool; pyridoxal (vitamin B6 analog) or metal ion catalysis; produces pyruvate as co-product enabling autocatalytic cycling

reactions.mechanism: Alanine transfers its amino group to alpha-ketoisovalerate via a Schiff base intermediate. The reaction proceeds through: (1) alanine forms a Schiff base with a carbonyl cofactor (pyridoxal analog or metal-bound carbonyl), (2) tautomerization shifts the double bond, (3) hydrolysis releases pyruvate from the alanine-derived fragment and generates the valine-bound Schiff base, (4) hydrolysis releases valine. Metal ions (Fe2+, Cu2+) catalyze the tautomerization step.
reactions.reasoning: Transamination is the dominant route to amino acids in modern metabolism and has been proposed as a prebiotically plausible reaction. Wachtershauser (1990) discussed transamination in the context of iron-sulfur world metabolism. The co-production of pyruvate (mol_009) creates an autocatalytic feedback loop where pyruvate feeds back into acetolactate formation (rxn_008), enhancing network robustness. Metal-ion-catalyzed transamination under prebiotic conditions has been studied by Zabinski and Toney (2001). This is the third terminal route to valine.

pathways.name: Direct FTT Strecker route via RWGS
pathways.description: CO2 is reduced to CO via the reverse water-gas shift on magnetite, CO undergoes Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis to produce isobutyraldehyde directly, then Strecker synthesis on montmorillonite clay yields valine via the aminonitrile intermediate.

pathways.name: Formate-CO-FTT Strecker route
pathways.description: CO2 is first reduced to formate on greigite, formate is then dehydrated to CO on mackinawite, CO enters FTT synthesis to produce isobutyraldehyde, and the Strecker route yields valine. This represents a two-step CO generation via the Wood-Ljungdahl analog.

pathways.name: Endogenous HCN + FTT Strecker route
pathways.description: CO is produced from CO2 via RWGS (rxn_003), then branches: one stream produces HCN from NH3+CO on pyrite (rxn_007), another stream produces isobutyraldehyde via FTT (rxn_004). HCN and isobutyraldehyde converge in the Strecker synthesis of valine aminonitrile.

pathways.name: Acetolactate route with FTT acetaldehyde and reductive amination
pathways.description: CO from RWGS is split: one stream yields pyruvate via FeS/H2S chemistry (rxn_005), another yields acetaldehyde via FTT (rxn_004). Pyruvate and acetaldehyde condense to acetolactate on montmorillonite (rxn_008), which rearranges to alpha-ketoisovalerate under UV (rxn_009). Reductive amination (rxn_014) yields valine.

pathways.name: Acetolactate route with FTT acetaldehyde and transamination
pathways.description: Identical to P4 through alpha-ketoisovalerate production, but terminates via transamination with alanine (rxn_015) instead of reductive amination. Alanine is produced in a parallel Strecker branch from FTT-derived acetaldehyde.

pathways.name: Pyruvate decarboxylation acetaldehyde route to acetolactate and reductive amination
pathways.description: CO produces pyruvate via FeS chemistry (rxn_005). Some pyruvate thermally decarboxylates to acetaldehyde on pyrite (rxn_006). A second pyruvate molecule condenses with this acetaldehyde to form acetolactate (rxn_008), which rearranges to alpha-ketoisovalerate (rxn_009). Reductive amination yields valine. This pathway uses pyruvate as both acetaldehyde source and condensation partner.

pathways.name: Full formate-derived acetolactate route with transamination
pathways.description: CO2 → formate (rxn_001) → CO (rxn_002) → pyruvate (rxn_005) and acetaldehyde (rxn_006 from second pyruvate). Acetolactate → alpha-ketoisovalerate → valine via transamination with Strecker-derived alanine. Entirely sourced from CO2/H2 through the formate intermediate.

pathways.name: Endogenous HCN + formate-CO-FTT Strecker route
pathways.description: A fully autotrophic route: CO2 → formate → CO (two steps), then CO branches to (a) produce isobutyraldehyde via FTT, (b) produce HCN via reaction with NH3. The endogenously produced HCN and isobutyraldehyde converge in the Strecker synthesis of valine.

pathways.name: Dual CO source convergent acetolactate-reductive amination route
pathways.description: CO is generated by both RWGS (rxn_003) and formate dehydration (rxn_001 + rxn_002), converging at the CO hub. CO then drives parallel pyruvate and acetaldehyde production feeding the acetolactate → alpha-ketoisovalerate → valine (reductive amination) pathway. Demonstrates environmental redundancy in CO supply.

pathways.name: Autocatalytic transamination loop with pyruvate recycling
pathways.description: The transamination of alpha-ketoisovalerate with alanine (rxn_015) produces valine + pyruvate. The regenerated pyruvate feeds back into acetolactate formation (rxn_008) with FTT-derived acetaldehyde, creating a proto-autocatalytic cycle. Alanine is continuously supplied from the Strecker branch. This pathway highlights the self-sustaining potential of the network.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Short, direct Strecker route. The main weakness is rxn_004: FTT synthesis producing branched-chain isobutyraldehyde is poorly supported. FTT typically yields linear aldehydes; branched C4 products are minor or undetected. HCN and NH3 are co-required from parallel reactions (rxn_007 or external NH3), which is fine topologically. Strecker steps are well-established (Friedmann & Miller 1969).
suggestions: Justify isobutyraldehyde from FTT with explicit selectivity data or add an aldol branching step from linear C2/C3 aldehydes
Specify FTT catalyst and temperature window that favors branched products

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Alternative CO source via formate adds robustness but also adds a step. Same FTT isobutyraldehyde issue as P1. Formate hydrogenation (rxn_001) and thermal dehydration (rxn_002) are both well-supported in literature. Slightly longer without additional chemical advantage over P1.
suggestions: Same as P1: isobutyraldehyde formation needs stronger justification

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Endogenous HCN production from NH3+CO is thermodynamically plausible at hydrothermal temperatures but kinetically limited; typically requires very high temperatures (>600°C) or specific catalysis. Including it makes the network more self-contained. Still carries the rxn_004 burden. Good environmental flow: all HCN is generated in situ before surface Strecker chemistry.
suggestions: Cite temperature regime and catalyst for HCN formation (e.g., >500°C on metal sulfides)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Metabolic-like route via acetolactate. Several weak links: (1) rxn_005 oversimplifies pyruvate from CO+H2S—this requires 3 CO insertions and is better described as a multi-step process; (2) rxn_009 UV-assisted decarboxylative rearrangement of acetolactate to alpha-ketoisovalerate is speculative—in biology this requires two enzymes (reductoisomerase + dehydratase) with NADPH; (3) rxn_014 reductive amination is listed without a reducing agent. This pathway mirrors the biosynthetic route but lacks experimental prebiotic evidence for the rearrangement step.
suggestions: Add explicit reductant (e.g., H2 or Fe2+) for rxn_014
Break rxn_005 into multi-step carbonyl insertion sequence
Provide literature support for UV-driven acetolactate rearrangement or replace with mineral-catalyzed alternative

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Complex route through acetolactate plus alanine-mediated transamination. Transamination (rxn_015) is better justified than reductive amination as it is known prebiotically with pyridoxal or metal-ion catalysis. However, the acetolactate→ketoisovalerate rearrangement (rxn_009) remains the bottleneck. The alanine synthesis arm via Strecker (rxn_012, rxn_013) is solid. Literature supports transamination as a prebiotic route (Keller et al.).
suggestions: Strengthen rxn_009 with experimental or computational evidence
Specify catalyst for transamination (e.g., pyridoxamine, metal ions)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Uses pyruvate decarboxylation for acetaldehyde—this is well-established (rxn_006). But inherits the problems of rxn_005 (pyruvate formation), rxn_009 (rearrangement), and rxn_014 (reductive amination without reductant). The internal recycling of acetaldehyde from pyruvate is chemically elegant but the weakest steps undermine the pathway.

Longest pathway (10 steps). Comprehensive but accumulates yield losses. Each weak step (rxn_005, rxn_009) further dilutes the overall probability. The formate route adds redundancy at the CO node but does not help the downstream bottlenecks.
suggestions: Simplify or provide stronger evidence for the acetolactate rearrangement

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Combines formate-CO and endogenous HCN paths into one Strecker route. Chemically similar to P2+P3 hybrid. Well-structured environmental flow. Isobutyraldehyde from FTT remains the weakest link.

Dual CO source is a nice redundancy feature but the downstream acetolactate rearrangement and reductive amination steps weaken the pathway. The convergence at CO is well-motivated but doesn't rescue the problematic later steps.
suggestions: Focus on strengthening downstream steps rather than adding CO redundancy

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The autocatalytic loop where pyruvate from rxn_015 feeds back into rxn_006 and rxn_008 is conceptually elegant and prebiotically meaningful—it mimics proto-metabolic cycling. However, the speculative rxn_009 and the questionable pyruvate synthesis (rxn_005) limit confidence. Transamination (rxn_015) is better supported than reductive amination and recycles pyruvate, adding coherence.
suggestions: This pathway would be the most interesting if rxn_009 had stronger support
Consider adding pyridoxal or metal-ion catalyst specification for transamination

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a well-constructed synthesis network with excellent topology: 5 hub molecules with degree ≥3, 10 diverse pathways, strong convergent redundancy at CO and acetaldehyde nodes, and a logical Hydrothermal→Surface→Biochemical environmental progression. The Strecker-based pathways (P1, P3) are the strongest and align well with literature (Friedmann & Miller 1969; Keefe et al. 1995). The metabolic-like acetolactate pathways (P4-P7, P9-P10) are creative and connect to extant biosynthesis but suffer from two key weaknesses: (1) rxn_009 (acetolactate → alpha-ketoisovalerate via UV rearrangement) lacks experimental prebiotic evidence—this is a complex enzymatic transformation in biology; (2) rxn_014 (reductive amination) is missing an explicit reducing agent. Additionally, rxn_004 (FTT synthesis of isobutyraldehyde) is a universal weak point—branched-chain aldehydes are not typical FTT products and would require an additional aldol branching mechanism. rxn_005 (pyruvate from CO + H2S) is oversimplified. Despite these issues, the network demonstrates strong strategic thinking: dual CO sources, dual acetaldehyde sources, three independent routes to valine, and a pyruvate recycling loop. The literature alignment is good for Strecker routes and moderate for the metabolic routes. With targeted improvements to rxn_004, rxn_005, rxn_009, and rxn_014, this network could achieve high confidence across all pathways.
